mission.title: Install and Configure Loxilb on Kubernetes
mission.description: loxilb is an eBPF-based cloud-native load balancer designed for Kubernetes, Edge, Telco, IoT, and XaaS environments. Installing loxilb allows you to manage load balancing effectively across various deployment scenarios, enhancing service communication within your Kubernetes clusters.

steps.title: Add the loxilb Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official loxilb Helm repository to your local Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add loxilb https://loxilb-io.github.io/loxilb-helm-repo
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install loxilb using Helm
steps.description:
Install loxilb in a new namespace called 'loxilb' with the following command:
```bash
helm install loxilb loxilb/loxilb --namespace loxilb --create-namespace --version v0.9.8.4
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the loxilb pods are running successfully with:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace loxilb
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
To access the loxilb service, set up port forwarding with:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/loxilb --namespace loxilb 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of loxilb will show the loxilb pods in a running state when you execute `kubectl get pods --namespace loxilb`. You will also be able to access the service via port 8080 on your local machine.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add loxilb https://loxilb-io.github.io/loxilb-helm-repo
helm repo update
helm install loxilb loxilb/loxilb --namespace loxilb --create-namespace --version v0.9.8.4

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the loxilb Helm release from the 'loxilb' namespace using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall loxilb --namespace loxilb
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with loxilb. First, delete the CRDs:
```bash
kubectl delete crd loxilb.*
```
Then, delete any PVCs in the 'loxilb' namespace:
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace loxilb
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'loxilb' namespace to clean up all resources:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace loxilb
```
Verify that the namespace has been removed:
```bash
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it is important to back up the current state of the loxilb installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace loxilb -o yaml > loxilb-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the loxilb installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade loxilb loxilb/loxilb --namespace loxilb --version v0.9.8.5
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check if the loxilb pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace loxilb
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the loxilb pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace loxilb
```
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the loxilb service, ensure that the port forwarding is set up correctly. Check the service status:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace loxilb
```
Verify that the correct ports are being forwarded.

troubleshooting.title: High resource usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high CPU or memory usage, check the resource limits set for the loxilb deployment:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment loxilb --namespace loxilb
```
Consider adjusting the resource limits in the deployment configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, ensure that you are using the correct namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace loxilb
```
If the release does not appear, it may not have been installed correctly.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and have installed Helm and kubectl.
